text: This is a robotic procedure for superior mesenteric artery syndrome, period. This patient is a thin, thirty-one-year-old female who's had six months of pain postprandially, and workup within the vascular service showed compression of the duodenum by her superior mesenteric artery, which you can see here. Both CT angiogram and flow studies showed that the velocities were consistent with obstruction of the duodenum. She's had pain postprandially for about six months with some weight loss. Some of the pain has been relieved with bending forward after eating period.

The surgeon reflects the transverse colon cephalad to easily access the duodenum, which is visible with a thin mesentery. The duodenum is tacked to the jejunum approximately 40 to 50 centimeters from the ligament of Treitz using a side-to-side approximation. Monopolar shears are used to create an aneurotomy in both the jejunum and duodenum, preparing for the placement of an endoscopic stapler through the openings.

The da Vinci 45mm stapler is inserted through the enterotomies in the jejunum and duodenum to perform a side-to-side anastomosis. Stay stitches are used to facilitate the alignment, and the stapler is clamped after adjusting the tissue to ensure the correct length and position for the anastomosis.

The surgeon removes the stapler and closes the common aneurotomy using a running 3-0 PDS suture, starting from both ends and tying it in the center. The closure is then reinforced with additional 3-0 Vicryl suture.
The surgeon places saline over the anastomosis closure and instills air through a nasogastric tube to test for leaks, confirming that no leaks are present.
